facts.fact: Index-only scans require pages to be marked 'all-visible' in the visibility map for PostgreSQL to skip heap access; if the visibility map bit is not set, the heap entry must be visited to determine visibility, negating performance gains.
Cardinality estimation errors in multi-join queries can be one order of magnitude or more, and errors propagate and amplify from the leaves to the root of the query plan.
PostgreSQL v12 introduced TID as part of the index key and truncation of redundant index attributes from internal pages, reducing index size while improving fan-out and page fitting.
PostgreSQL v13 introduced index deduplication by default, storing repeated values once per page and maintaining a compact structure that tracks all matching heap pointers.
CERT (Cardinality Estimation Restriction Testing) found 13 unique and previously unknown bugs in MySQL, CockroachDB, and TiDB by detecting inconsistent estimated cardinalities in query plans.

In EXPLAIN ANALYZE output, the first number in cost (e.g., 0.00 in 0.00..19.00) is startup cost, and the second is total cost; these are arbitrary units conventionally thought of as disk page fetches.
Window functions optimized with CTEs and PARTITION BY indexes can achieve single-pass efficiency, reducing query time by two orders of magnitude (e.g., 8 seconds to 10ms) when properly indexed.
PostgreSQL 18 introduces B-tree skip scan capability to overcome the long-standing left-most index limitation, improving performance for queries with non-leading column predicates.
Heap fetches during an index-only scan indicate that some data wasn't in the index and had to be fetched from the table; a high number suggests the index is not covering all columns needed.
PostgreSQL covering indexes can be created using the INCLUDE clause to add non-key columns, enabling index-only scans without heap fetches for those columns.
SQL Server 2025 introduces Cardinality Estimation Feedback for Expressions, which learns from repeated executions to adjust cardinality estimates for subexpressions across queries.
pg_hint_plan allows embedding optimizer hints as comments (/*+ hint */) without altering SQL structure, enabling specification of join methods, scan methods, and planner parameters.

Uber's architecture separates the stateless query engine layer (query planning, routing, sharding) from the storage engine layer (consensus, replication, transactions), with CacheFront integrated caching reducing query latency.
PostgreSQL from v14 onward sometimes skips index cleanup when only a small percentage of pages contain dead rows, preventing those pages from becoming all-visible even after VACUUM.

Modern main-memory databases like DuckDB and HEAVY.AI operate with limited or even no cardinality estimation yet achieve competitive performance, suggesting evolving perspectives on traditional cardinality estimation necessity.

examples.example: Netflix uses Cassandra, CockroachDB, and EV Cache for different data access patterns, with Apache Kafka and Flink for real-time analytics, demonstrating the trade-off between query optimization and polyglot persistence.
examples.context: Database selection for analytics and transaction handling

examples.example: Uber's Docstore reported that more than 50% of queries were ReadRows requests, which became the primary target for CacheFront caching integration to optimize repeated read patterns.
examples.impact: Reduced latency by integrated caching at query engine layer

examples.example: A query with an improper window function without PARTITION BY index caused 8-second response times; adding index on PARTITION BY field and refactoring with CTEs reduced time to 10ms—200x improvement.

CERT discovered 13 bugs in MySQL, CockroachDB, and TiDB by analyzing cardinality estimate inconsistencies—e.g., a LEFT JOIN had a higher estimated cardinality than the equivalent INNER JOIN, violating logical constraints.
PostgreSQL 17 skip-scan optimization on IN(...) lists showed 20-30% improvement in CPU-bound cases where previously the optimizer had to start at the left-most index value.

analogies.analogy: The visibility map is like a sticky note on each page of a book that says 'all readers have seen this page'—the database only needs to check the note, not re-read the entire page.
Cardinality estimation is like a census prediction before the census is taken—if your estimate is off by an order of magnitude, your budget planning (query plan) will be wildly wrong.
analogies.concept: Cardinality estimation impact on query planning

analogies.analogy: A covering index is like having a one-page summary of a book instead of needing to walk to the library to fetch the full book—if the summary has all the info you need, you never leave your desk.
The B-tree skip scan is like being able to jump to page 47 in a book using the index instead of having to start from the bookmark on page 1 and flip forward.
Heap fetches during an index-only scan are like finding the address in a phone book but still having to visit the house—the optimization partially failed.

key_numbers.context: Multi-join queries routinely produce errors of 10-100x between estimated and actual cardinality

PostgreSQL 13+ deduplicates repeated values within single index page by default

common_misconceptions.misconception: An index-only scan always avoids heap access and is therefore always faster than an index scan.
common_misconceptions.reality: Index-only scans still require heap fetches if visibility map pages are not marked all-visible, making them no faster than regular index scans in stale visibility maps.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Higher query plan costs always mean longer execution time.
common_misconceptions.reality: Cost is an arbitrary unit conventionally thought of as disk page fetches; actual execution time depends on many factors including CPU, I/O, cache behavior, and system load.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Window functions always cause full-table scans and should be avoided.
common_misconceptions.reality: Window functions with proper PARTITION BY indexes and CTEs can achieve single-pass efficiency; the problem is incorrect use, not the feature itself.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All cardinality estimation errors impact query performance equally.
common_misconceptions.reality: Errors are most damaging in multi-join queries where they propagate and amplify from leaves to root; errors in single-table scans have minimal impact.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Query hints should be used to override optimizer decisions whenever the optimizer is wrong.
common_misconceptions.reality: Hints should only be used as temporary stopgaps while root causes are resolved; PostgreSQL philosophy favors query rewrites, statistics updates, and index design.

common_misconceptions.misconception: VACUUM is a maintenance task that doesn't need to be tuned—just run it periodically.
common_misconceptions.reality: Autovacuum frequency directly impacts the visibility map freshness; insufficient vacuuming leaves pages unmarked as all-visible, causing index-only scans to perform heap fetches.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All databases need sophisticated cardinality estimation to perform well.
common_misconceptions.reality: Modern main-memory databases like DuckDB achieve competitive performance with minimal or no cardinality estimation due to different execution strategies (vectorization, JIT compilation).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Covering indexes are always better than regular indexes for queries that need multiple columns.
common_misconceptions.reality: Covering indexes trade write performance (larger indexes, more maintenance overhead) for read optimization; the tradeoff is worthwhile only for frequently-executed read-heavy queries.
